elements.sent-2411-001: Serre, in his paper \cite{1 , established a sufficient condition on $n$ for the $n$-th coefficient of the series $\eta^{26}$ to vanish.}
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the question that whether this is a necessary condition remained unanswered.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, using the theory of Hecke eigenforms explored by Serre, we prove some partial cases for the converse part.
elements.sent-2411-004: The Dedekind's eta-function is defined by \begin{equation}\eta(z):=q^{1/24}\prod_{m=1}^\infty(1-q^m),\end{equation} where $q=e^{2\pi iz}$, $\Im(z)>0$.
elements.sent-2411-005: For an integer $r$, the $r$-th power of $\eta$ can be expressed as the infinite sum \begin{equation}\eta^r(z)=q^{r/24}\sum_{n=0}^\infty p_r(n)q^n,\end{equation} where the coefficients $p_r(n)$ are defined by the identity \begin{equation}\prod_{m=1}^\infty (1-q^m)^r=\sum_{n=0}^\infty p_r(n)q^n.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-006: There has been much interest in $p_r(n)$, in particular in its vanishing.
elements.sent-2411-007: It turns out that, for certain values of the exponent $r$, the set of $n$ such that $p_r(n)\neq0$ has density zero.
elements.sent-2411-008: In that case, the series $\eta^r$ is said to be lacunary.
elements.sent-2411-009: This is the case for $r=1,3$ as shown by the identities of Euler and Jacobi, respectively (\cite{3 , Ch: XIX): \begin{equation}\prod_{m=1}^\infty(1-q^m) =\sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty(-1)^nq^{(3n^2+n)/2}=1-q-q^2+q^5+q^7-q^{12}-q^{15}+q^{22}+q^{26}-\cdots, \prod_{m=1}^\infty(1-q^m)^3 =\sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty(-1)^n(2n+1)q^{(n^2+n)/2}=1-3q+5q^3-7q^6+9q^{10}-11q^{15}+13q^{21}-\cdots.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Computer calculations make it probable that there is no other odd values of $r$ for which the series $\eta^r$ is lacunary.
elements.sent-2411-011: On the other hand, for even $r$, Ramanujan \cite{4 proved that $\eta^r$ is lacunary for $r=2,4,6,8$.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Later, it was proved (\cite{5,6,7 ) that this is also the case for $r=10,14\text{ and 26$.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Finally, Serre \cite{1 proved that these are the only cases for even $r$ such that $\eta^r$ is lacunary.}
elements.sent-2411-014: For $r=2,4,6,8,10\text{ and 14$, Serre \cite{1 also established necessary and sufficient conditions on $n$ so that $p_r(n)=0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Furthermore, for $r=26$, he proved the following theorem:.
elements.sent-2411-016: There exists a prime number $p\equiv3\pmod4$ whose highest power dividing $12n+13$ is odd; and there exists a prime number $p'\equiv2\pmod3$ whose highest power dividing $12n+13$ is odd.
elements.sent-2411-017: This theorem is also proved by Chan et.
elements.sent-2411-018: al. \cite{2 by establishing an explicit formula for $p_{26 (n)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, whether the converse is true or not remained unanswered.
elements.sent-2411-020: Let us denote the highest power of a prime number $p$ dividing an integer $n$ as $ord_p(n)$.
elements.sent-2411-021: Note that the following statement is the converse of Theorem~\ref{Serre theorem .}
elements.sent-2411-022: There exists a prime number $p\equiv3\pmod4$ such that $ord_p(12n+13)$ is odd; and there exists a prime number $p'\equiv2\pmod3$ such that $ord_{p' (12n+13)$ is odd.}
elements.sent-2411-023: For every prime $p\equiv 3 \pmod 4$, $\mathrm{ord _p(12n+13)$ is even and there exists a prime $p^\prime \not\equiv 11 \pmod {12}$ such that $\mathrm{ord _{p^\prime (12n+13)>0.$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: For every prime $p\equiv 2 \pmod 3$, $\mathrm{ord _p(12n+13)$ is even and there exists a prime $p^\prime \not\equiv 11 \pmod {12}$ such that $\mathrm{ord _{p^\prime (12n+13)>0.$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Finally as applications of those results, we prove the following necessary and sufficient conditions.
elements.sent-2411-026: Let $n\in \mathbb{N}$ such that $\ord_p(12n+1)\not\equiv 4 \pmod 5$ for every prime $p\equiv 1 \pmod {12}$.
elements.sent-2411-027: Then $p_{26 (25n+1)=0$ if and only if there exists a prime number $p\equiv 3 \pmod 4$ such that $\ord_p(12n+1)$ is odd; and there exists a prime number $p^\prime \equiv 2 \pmod 3$ such that $\ord_{p^\prime (12n+1)$ is odd.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Let $n\in \mathbb{N}$ such that $\ord_p(12n+1)\not\equiv 6 \pmod 7$ for every prime $p\equiv 1 \pmod {12}$.
elements.sent-2411-029: Then $p_{26 (49n+3)=0$ if and only if there exists a prime number $p\equiv 3 \pmod 4$ such that $\ord_p(12n+1)$ is odd; and there exists a prime number $p^\prime \equiv 2 \pmod 3$ such that $\ord_{p^\prime (12n+1)$ is odd.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Let us consider an imaginary quadratic field $K=\mathbb Q(\sqrt d)$ with discriminant $d$.
elements.sent-2411-031: We denote the ring of integers of $K$ by $\mathcal O_K$.
elements.sent-2411-032: Suppose, $c$ is a Hecke character on $K$ of exponent $k-1$ and conductor $\mathfrak f_c$, where $\mathfrak f_c$ is a nonzero ideal of $\mathcal O_K$.
elements.sent-2411-033: Now we define the series \begin{equation}\phi_{K,c}(z):=\sum_{\mathfrak a}c(\mathfrak a)q^{N(\mathfrak a)}\quad(q:=e^{2\pi iz}, \Im(z)>0),\end{equation} where the sum is taken over all ideals $\mathfrak a$ of $\mathcal O_K$ which are coprime to $\mathfrak f_c$.
elements.sent-2411-034: We have that $\phi_{K,c (z)$ is a normalized Hecke eigenform of weight $k$, level $|d|\cdot N(\mathfrak f_c)$ and character $\epsilon_K\omega_c$, where $\epsilon_K$ and $\omega_c$ are Dirichlet characters defined as \begin{equation}\epsilon_K(p)=\left(\frac{d}{p}\right) for primes p such that p\nmid2d\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\omega_c(n)=\frac{c(n\mathcal O_K)}{n^{k-1}} for integers n such that the ideal n\mathcal O_K is coprime to \mathfrak f_c.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\epsilon_K(p)=\left(\frac{d}{p}\right) for primes p such that p\nmid2d\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\omega_c(n)=\frac{c(n\mathcal O_K)}{n^{k-1}} for integers n such that the ideal n\mathcal O_K is coprime to \mathfrak f_c.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-035: For more details please refer to \cite{1 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Firstly, consider the number field $K_1=\mathbb Q(\sqrt{-3 )$ of discriminant $-3$ and the ideal $\mathfrak f_1=4\sqrt{-3}\cdot\mathcal O_{K_1}$ of norm $48$.}
elements.sent-2411-037: We now define Hecke characters $c_{1\pm}$ on $K_1$.
elements.sent-2411-038: Let $\mathfrak a$ be an ideal of $\mathcal O_{K_1}$ coprime to $\mathfrak f_1$.
elements.sent-2411-039: Since $\mathcal O_{K_1}$ is a principal ideal domain, we may choose a generator $a=x+y\sqrt{-3}$ of $\mathfrak a$ with $x,y\in\mathbb Z$, $x+y\equiv1\pmod2$ and $x\equiv1\pmod3$.. We define \begin{equation}c_{1\pm}(\mathfrak a)=(-1)^{(x\mp y-1)/2}a^{12}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-040: It can be easily verified that $c_{1\pm}$ thus defined are Hecke characters on $K_1$ of exponent $12$ and conductor $\mathfrak f_1$, and that $\epsilon_{K_1}\omega_{c_{1+ =\left(\frac{-1 {\bullet}\right)=\epsilon_{K_1}\omega_{c_{1- }$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Next, consider the number field $K_2=\mathbb Q(i)$ of discriminant $-4$ and the ideal $\mathfrak f_2=6\cdot\mathcal O_{K_2}$ of norm $36$.
elements.sent-2411-042: We now define Hecke characters $c_{2\pm}$ on $K_2$.
elements.sent-2411-043: Let $\mathfrak b$ be an ideal of $\mathcal O_{K_2}$ coprime to $\mathfrak f_2$.
elements.sent-2411-044: Since $\mathcal O_{K_2}$ is a principal ideal domain, we may choose a generator $b$ of $\mathfrak b$ and determine $m\in\mathbb Z/2\mathbb Z$ and $n\in\mathbb Z/8\mathbb Z$ such that \begin{equation}b\equiv i^m\pmod{2\cdot\mathcal O_2} \quad and \quad b\equiv(1-i)^n\pmod{3\cdot\mathcal O_2}.\end{equation}. We define \begin{equation}c_{2\pm}(\mathfrak b)=(-1)^{3m}(\pm i)^{3n}b^{12}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-045: It can be easily verified that $c_{2\pm}$ thus defined are Hecke characters on $K_2$ of exponent $12$ and conductor $\mathfrak f_2$, and that $\epsilon_{K_2}\omega_{c_{2+ =\left(\frac{-1 {\bullet}\right)=\epsilon_{K_2}\omega_{c_{2- }$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: By \cite{1 , we have \begin{equation}\eta^{26}(12z)=\frac{1}{32617728}\left(\phi_{K_1,c_{1+}}(z)+\phi_{K_1,c_{1-}}(z)-\phi_{K_2,c_{2+}}(z)-\phi_{K_2,c_{2-}}(z)\right),\end{equation} and so $\eta^{26 (12z)$ is a linear combination of forms of CM-type of weight $13$, level $144$ and character $\left(\frac{-1 {\bullet}\right)$.. We write \begin{equation}\phi_{K_1,c_{1\pm}}(z)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty t_{1\pm}(n)q^n \quad and \quad \phi_{K_2,c_{2\pm}}(z)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty t_{2\pm}(n)q^n.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Comparing the coefficients of the terms involving $q^{12n+13}$, we get \begin{equation}p_{26}(n)=\frac{1}{32617728}\big(t_{1+}(12n+13)+t_{1-}(12n+13)-t_{2+}(12n+13)-t_{2-}(12n+13)\big)\label{eq:5}\end{equation} for every nonnegative integer $n$.
elements.sent-2411-049: Let the prime decomposition of $12n+13$ be given by \begin{equation}12n+13=\prod_{prime \ p} p^{\alpha_p}, \ where \ \alpha_p:=ord_p(12n+13).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-050: In view of the properties of Hecke eigenforms, we have \begin{equation}t_{1\pm}(12n+13)=\prod_p t_{1\pm}(p^{\alpha_p}) \quad and \quad t_{2\pm}(12n+13)=\prod_p t_{2\pm}(p^{\alpha_p}),\label{eq:8}\end{equation} Let us have a look at $t_{1+ (p^\alpha)$, $t_{1- (p^\alpha)$, $t_{2+ (p^\alpha)$, $t_{2- (p^\alpha)$ for a prime $p\geq5$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Let $t$ denote any one among $t_{1\pm}$ and $t_{2\pm}$.
elements.sent-2411-052: By the properties of Hecke eigenforms, if $p\equiv1\pmod4$, then \begin{equation}\label{Hecke relation for 1 mod 4} t(p^r)=t(p)t(p^{r-1})-p^{12}t(p^{r-2})\end{equation} for any integer $r\geq 2$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Equivalently for any $\beta\geq0$, \begin{equation}t(p^{2\beta}) =t(p)^{2\beta}-\binom{2\beta-1}{1}p^{12}t(p)^{2\beta-2}+\dots+(-1)^{\beta-1}\binom{\beta+1}{2}p^{12\beta-12}t(p)^2+(-1)^\beta p^{12\beta},\label{eq:1} t(p^{2\beta+1}) =t(p)^{2\beta+1}-\binom{2\beta}{1}p^{12}t(p)^{2\beta-1}+\dots+(-1)^{\beta-1}\binom{\beta+2}{3}p^{12\beta-12}t(p)^3+(-1)^\beta\binom{\beta+1}{1}p^{12\beta}t(p).\label{eq:2}\end{equation} On the other hand if $p\equiv3\pmod4$ then \begin{equation}\label{Hecke relation for 3 mod 4} t(p^r)=t(p)t(p^{r-1})+p^{12}t(p^{r-2})\end{equation} for any integer $r\geq 2$.

elements.sent-2411-258: In this section we always assume that $n\in \mathbb{N}$ such that $\ord_p(12n+1)\not\equiv 4 \pmod 5$ for every prime $p\equiv 1 \pmod {12}$.
elements.sent-2411-259: Furthermore, if n is such that there exists a prime number $p\equiv 3 \pmod 4$ such that $\ord_p(12n+1)$ is odd (equivalently $\ord_p(12(25n+1)+13)$ is odd) and there exists a prime number $p^\prime \equiv 2 \pmod 3$ such that $\ord_{p^\prime (12n+1)$ is odd (equivalently $\ord_{p^\prime (12(25n+1)+13)$ is odd), then from Theorem \ref{Serre theorem it follows that $p_{26 (25n+1)=0$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-260: Hence it suffices to prove the converse part.
elements.sent-2411-261: Note that the converse part of Theorem \ref{MT 1 is equivalent to prove the following theorem:.}
elements.sent-2411-262: If $n$ satisfies one of the following conditions, then $p_{26 (25n+1)\ne 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-265: Let $\ord_p(12n+1)$ be even for every prime $p\equiv 3\pmod 4$.
elements.sent-2411-266: Hence $\ord_p(12(25n+1)+13)$ is even for every prime $p\equiv 3 \pmod 4$.
elements.sent-2411-267: If there exists a prime number $p'\equiv 5\pmod{12}$ such that $\ord_{p' (12(25n+1)+13)$ is odd, then the result follows from Theorem \ref{power of primes 5 mod 12 is odd .}}

elements.sent-2411-269: Let $\ord_p(12n+1)$ be even for every prime $p\equiv 2 \pmod 3$.
elements.sent-2411-270: Hence $\ord_p(12(25n+1)+13)$ is even for every prime $p\equiv 2 \pmod 3$.
elements.sent-2411-271: If there exists a prime $p^\prime \equiv 7 \pmod {12}$ such that $\ord(12(25n+1)+13)$ is odd, then the result follows from Theorem \ref{power of primes 7 mod 12 is odd .}
